Round 623 — the doc gate: a documented v.publishable_decision_summary: The generalization R620 named and did not build, and R622 carried: a gate for the docs. R620 and R622 fixed real doc defects — a config key dead since Round 400 shipping in five copy-paste blocks ~220 rounds later, both READMEs' quickstart failing to parse, a CI example invoking a verb deleted with GENERATED.md, a _vN-banned identifier living inside two docs — and every one of them was found by a person reading. That is the same posture that let them rot in the first place, so the round is not done until the question is decided by a machine. Two questions about tracked docs are decidable and are now gated: a verb a reader is told to run must dispatch, and a mnemosyne.toml a reader is told to paste must parse through the real loader. The verb oracle is --help, which R622 made a projection of the COMMANDS table, so the gate cannot drift from dispatch. IT IMMEDIATELY EARNED ITSELF: both READMEs documented a CLI commit verb recording design-doc transactions to RocksDB column families — it has never dispatched, and mnemosyne-store is not even a CLI dependency. Three independent adversarial reviewers and two rounds of hand-sweeping missed it; the gate named it in one run. Getting it precise mattered more than getting it built: the first parser read cargo's own --force as a verb, the second cut --help to help, the third mis-paired fence backticks with prose, and the fourth silently MISSED the English commit mention because the inline span wrapped a line while catching the Korean one — an under-detecting verification path, the exact failure this arc condemns. A gate that cries wolf gets ignored and a gate that under-detects is worse than none, so both floors are counted and the non-vacuity is proven by injection.
publishable_changes_bullets: new tests/docs_match_reality_smoke.rs: every mnemosyne-cli <verb> a tracked doc tells a reader to run must dispatch, and every fenced mnemosyne.toml must parse through the real mnemosyne_config::parse_config
verb set is read from --help, which R622 made a projection of the COMMANDS table — so the gate's oracle cannot drift from dispatch

scope is INSTRUCTION only: shell-fence lines + inline-code spans. Prose about the tool is excluded (routes through `mnemosyne-cli` mutate API must not read `mutate` as a verb); frozen ledger (docs/.atomic) and claudedocs experiment records are excluded as accurate history, not drift
inline spans are scanned over the whole prose body, not per line — a span wrapping a newline is one span in rendered markdown and a per-line scan silently misses it
config gate checks only blocks carrying [workspace] (the loader requires it); a lone [atomic] override block is a documented fragment, not a paste-whole config

FOUND AND FIXED by the gate, missed by three reviewers and by hand: both READMEs documented `mnemosyne-cli commit` recording transactions to RocksDB column families — no such verb has ever dispatched, and mnemosyne-store is not a CLI dependency at all
publishable_verification_bullets: gate is NON-VACUOUS by injection: a planted `mnemosyne-cli totally-fake-verb` line in a tracked doc is caught by file:line, and the gate returns green when reverted

gate is PRECISE: 0 false positives across all tracked instructional markdown. Three parser defects were found and fixed before landing — cargo's own --force/--test flags read as verbs, `mnemosyne-cli --` markers cut `--help` to `help`, and fence backticks mis-paired with prose backticks

Round 624 — a config section that exists.decision_summary: The last gateable question in the R620-R624 discoverability arc: a config section that exists must be NAMED by the guides. R623 gated what a doc tells you to RUN; this gates what it tells you the software HAS. The oracle is derived, not restated — WorkspaceConfig's every field is serde(default) with no skip_serializing_if, so serializing a minimal parse yields all 13 section names at runtime, and a hand-written list would have been exactly one more copy of the thing this arc exists to delete. It earned itself immediately: the MCP guide an agent auto-loads named 4 of 13 sections and omitted [continuity] — the narrative gate's own config — which is a mechanical restatement of the consumer's error #7 (they believed validate-workspace was their narrative gate, never learned validate-continuity was configurable, saw green, and shipped an unchecked world). docs/SCHEMA_GUIDE.md, the full field-level reference, documented [[publishable_override_ledger]] zero times. Both fixed, from the type and from the repo's own working entries rather than from memory. Precision was again the hard part and again caught before landing: the first check would have falsely flagged [plugins] (documented as its subtable) and the two Vec sections (written [[name]]), and a gate that cries wolf gets ignored as surely as one that under-detects. HONEST RESIDUE: the bootstrap skill an adopter meets FIRST lives outside this repo and no gate here can see it, while still teaching GENERATED.md and emitting an unparseable config; and two resources remain oriented to the spec half only, which is a judgment call no gate decides.
changes_bullets: new gate schema_guides_name_every_config_section: both schema guides must NAME every WorkspaceConfig section. The section list is DERIVED from the type's serde shape at runtime (every field is serde(default) with no skip_serializing_if, so serializing a minimal parse emits all 13) — no hand-written list, which would be one more copy of the class this arc deletes
the gate accepts all three TOML spellings a section can be documented under: plain table, array-of-tables for Vec sections, and subtable

MCP resources/schema-guide.md gains the 7 sections it never named — chiefly [continuity], the narrative gate's OWN config (canon_order_path / rules_path / severity / interval_severity), plus [atomic] [plugins] [spec_drift] [content_drift] [commit_ledger] [verifies_catalog] [[orphan_ledger]] [[publishable_override_ledger]] [terminology]; its Validation section now names validate_continuity beside validate_workspace

derived-oracle floor asserted (>10 sections) so a serde/reflection regression fails loud rather than passing empty
the derivation was PROVEN before use, not assumed: a throwaway probe confirmed a minimal parse serializes all 13 section keys

the first cut of the check was too strict and would have shipped 2 false positives ([plugins] documented as [plugins.set_equality_validator], Vec sections as [[name]]) — caught and fixed before landing, because a gate that cries wolf gets ignored

the STATE-EXPRESSION GUARD (R621's corrected residue of R527 gap #2) is still un-owned and still answerable at code 0 via the R559 refute-or-design pattern. Round 625 — the state-expression guard R.decision_summary: R625 answers field-report 3c — the question R547/R560/R620 each dodged — and REFUTES its own proposed build. THE RULING: R560's body is the charter (a guard CONDITION over world-state is Mnemosyne's; only side-effecting execution is SCE). The conflict was REAL and UNRULED: R621 CORRECTION 5 concedes R560 never PERCEIVED 'the conflict with R547's body', and the consumer read the same conflict off the ledger independently; R547's delegation rests on mis-citing R546, which had put cond-guards on the world-state side. APPLIED to the consumer's 9 MEASURED conds: 7/9 (mouth_confessed) are ALREADY store-derived through their own seam and are NOT drift; 1/9 (on_the_flat) is adaptation-side; 1/9 (unanswered >= 3) is an ACCUMULATING COUNTER that R560's own carve-out puts pinion-side, so their hand-authored cond is CORRECT. THEREFORE NO MNEMOSYNE BUILD: the one class they cannot write is exactly the class R560 already assigns to pinion. Gap 3c's premise stands CONFIRMED (it asked about the STATE-EXPRESSION class, not about gates — the store holds ZERO numeric typed scalars and f-tide-slips carries its law as prose only), which I had backwards. MY DESIGN (TypedObject::Fact + PredicateObjectKind::Fact) was REFUTED before build by 3 independent adversarial reviewers: it compiles clean with 293/293 green while half-wired, its migration step is UNEXECUTABLE (no set_predicate/remove_predicate; a consumer following it BRICKS their predicate unrecoverably), it miscites the half-enforced-invariant anti-pattern onto a leg whose parity HOLDS, and the consumer had already DECIDED the scalar deliberately citing R616 decision C and shipped the check (knots.py G4) in the SAME COMMIT as the predicate. The REAL half-enforced invariant is elsewhere and 60x bigger: disclosure overrides hold 1000 fact refs, set-disclosure REJECTS a missing fact, retract-fact ORPHANS one silently — the write path forbids exactly what the delete path creates. LEDGER CORRECTION (6th of the R621 class): R620's 'never used requires' is FALSE (14 uses, verified on the tracked store as of the commit an hour before the report); its other two grounds survive, so re-author-then-re-measure stands.
changes_bullets: MY OWN DESIGN REFUTED before build by 3 independent adversarial reviewers (the R613/R616 pattern; the refutation IS the deliverable). I proposed TypedObject::Fact + PredicateObjectKind::Fact to give the consumer's 16 fact-referencing guards referential integrity. Reviewer B BUILT the patch: the workspace compiles clean and 293/293 tests pass with the variant half-wired, and a consumer following my migration step BRICKS their predicate — add-predicate --object-kind fact is ACCEPTED, then no fact can use it, and with no set_predicate/remove_predicate it is UNRECOVERABLE via the mutate API. My step 5 was unexecutable and I did not know.

THE RULING (the thing actually asked, punted by R547/R560/R620, now issued at code 0): R560's body IS the charter. The conflict is REAL and was UNRULED — R621 CORRECTION 5 says R560 never PERCEIVED "the conflict with R547's body", which concedes the conflict exists while denying any round found it; the consumer independently read the same conflict off the ledger (field report 3c quotes both). R547's delegation clause rests on a mis-citation of R546 (R546 put cond-guards on the world-state side; R547 restated that as "cond = executable behavior"), so R560 is later, precise, and correct: a guard CONDITION over world-state is Mnemosyne's; only side-effecting execution is SCE.

THE RULING APPLIED to the consumer's 9 MEASURED conds (not to the hypothetical): 7/9 mouth_confessed are ALREADY store-derived through their own seam (store opened_by+completed_by then knots.py then knots.json then engine then event payload then cond; the derived artifact byte-matches a regeneration from the pristine store) = they already implement R560 correctly, UNAIDED, and it is NOT drift; 1/9 on_the_flat comes from tide-table.json with zero store reads = adaptation-side, their call; 1/9 unanswered >= 3 is an ACCUMULATING COUNTER, which R560's OWN carve-out puts pinion-side/optional/deferred = their hand-authored cond is CORRECT, not drift. THEREFORE NO MNEMOSYNE BUILD for gap 3c: the one class they cannot write is exactly the class R560 already assigns to pinion.

3c's PREMISE stands CONFIRMED, not refuted (I had it backwards, and one reviewer with me): it asked about the STATE-EXPRESSION class ("if trust >= N"), not about gates. The consumer authored the 16 opened_by gates on 07-14 and wrote 3c on 07-15 — they knew what they had written the day before, and scoped 3c precisely to the class they could NOT write. Measured: f-tide-slips carries the "three unanswered calls" law as PROSE ONLY with typed leg None; the 852-fact store holds ZERO numeric typed scalars; 15 facts whose prose says "three" are all untyped.

THE CONSUMER WAS A DECIDER, NOT A VICTIM — my framing was wrong and their source says so. opened_by's scalar is a DOCUMENTED decision citing OUR OWN ruling (knots.py:13-18 + build-store.sh: "which fact opens this game's knot is the adaptation consumer's concern, so it is not a place to grow the schema (GAP-005 decision C) ... this gate closes that gap"), and they shipped the gate (knots.py G4 = verbatim my proposed check, fail-loud) in the SAME COMMIT (ed4f134) that introduced the predicate. The gap never existed. Their G5 (is the opening fact diggable before the ritual) is STRONGER than my proposal: existence never broke, satisfiability did.

LEDGER CORRECTION (6th of the R621 class, load-bearing): R620 wrote "the consumer ... never used requires" and used it to ground "do NOT build a condition primitive yet". FALSE — 14 typed uses. The store is TRACKED; at the consumer commit contemporaneous with the field report (da060bd, one hour before it) requires=14 and opened_by=16. R620 measured typed 9.7% (83/852) correctly off that same store and got this item wrong. R620's other two grounds (typed 9.7%, surface.object 0/1000) SURVIVE, so the re-author-then-re-measure order stands.

MY OWN BRIEF'S ERRORS, recorded rather than quietly dropped: the founding premise ("R560 reversed R547") re-asserted the exact reading R621 retracted ONE ROUND EARLIER = the R620 shape repeated by its own corrector; "half-enforced invariant" was a MISCITATION (the typed leg has 3 write paths all routed through build_typed_claim, parity HOLDS, and the scalar leg does exactly what it documents); provenance wrong (opened_by = ed4f134 07-14, not ba907a3 07-13; ba907a3 was requires); "3 gates depend on f-jiun-bloodline" was 1 (I read the subject's outbound count as the target's inbound).
verification_bullets: EVERY reviewer claim re-run BY ME before it entered this ledger (R620's failure mode was writing an unverified claim; a reviewer is not an oracle either). Two reviewer claims were REJECTED on that re-run: one held R620's "never used requires" referred to the report's content rather than the store (the verbatim R620 text — "the consumer ... has typed 9.7% of facts, used surface.object 0 times, and never used requires" — is about the store, and the third reviewer independently agreed with me); one held there is "no charter conflict to rule on", which overreads R621 CORRECTION 5 ("never perceiving the conflict with R547's body" concedes the conflict).

MEASURED on the real 852-fact consumer store, never a fixture, copied to scratch and never mutating the consumer repo (its git status stayed clean): 9 cond= attrs over 3 vars (mouth_confessed x7, on_the_flat x1, unanswered >= 3 x1); typed 83/852 = 9.7%; predicates requires=14 / opened_by=16 / completed_by=22 / pursues=22; 24 scalar objects split cleanly into 16 that resolve to real fact ids and 8 genuine opaque vocabulary; trust = 0 occurrences store-wide (the consumer's own audit records village-trust as designed-but-not-built) so the RESUME's blocking question rested on a hypothetical.

INJECTION-PROVEN (non-vacuity, the standing rule): add-fact with opened_by pointing at a nonexistent fact = ACCEPTED exit 0 with validate-workspace and validate-continuity both exit 0; the same shape via requires (entity leg) = REJECTED exit 1; retract-fact on a gate's target = SUCCEEDS, leaving the gate dangling, both gates still green. The hole is real. What the review established is that it is NOT a fault the consumer suffers: knots.py G4 catches it fail-loud before engine/ can consume knots.json.

CARRY-1 VERIFIED BY ME e2e, not taken from the reviewers: disclosure overrides hold 1000 fact refs (reader 836 / reader-flee 81 / reader-substitute 83); retract-fact on an override's target SUCCEEDS exit 0 and leaves the override ORPHANED with both validators green; set-disclosure then REFUSES to create that same state ("fact not present in narrative_facts", exit 1). The write path forbids exactly what the delete path creates = the canonical CLAUDE.md anti-pattern, at 1000 refs against my typed leg's 16.

CARRY-2 VERIFIED BY ME e2e on a purpose-built store: a requires edge declared object_kind=scalar IMPORTS CLEAN (exit 0) and report-quest-graph returns prerequisites=[] for the quest that authored it — the edge is SILENTLY DROPPED at the if-let Entity in continuity.rs, while structural_fact_ids matches the predicate STRING and still counts it, so the two disagree. The quest contract's object_shape is prose, not enforcement. Live TODAY, independent of this round.

Citation hygiene (R255): all 18 cited rounds verified present in the changelog before writing. My first check reported R255/R568/R585 ABSENT — a false negative from my own trailing-space prefix, the exact class R620 shipped when list_sections answered "hallucinated" for all 366 rounds. Doubted the tool, re-ran, all 18 present. Ledger 252..624, 371 entries.

Code 0 (the RULEBOOK DESIGN round): no crate, schema, or gate touched. Deliverable = this self-contained entry (R452). Nothing is claimed green that was not run; no build was made, so no build is claimed.
carry_forward_bullets: NEXT = R626, and it is NOT this round's refuted design: CLOSE THE REAL HALF-ENFORCED INVARIANT — add disclosure overrides to inbound_fact_refs (atomic/lib.rs:4982-5002 scans conflicts_with / supersedes_in_frame / pays_off and never touches disclosure_plans). 1000 live refs, the write path already enforces it, no schema change, no migration, no consumer ask, and it is the brief's own principle applied where it actually holds.

CARRY (live bug, R627 candidate): the if-let Entity silent-drop at continuity.rs:4178/4187 — a requires/pursues edge declared object_kind=scalar imports clean and vanishes from report-quest-graph while structural_fact_ids still counts it. Either enforce quest predicates' object_kind at write time or fail loud on a shape the projection cannot read; do not leave two functions disagreeing.

CARRY (prerequisite for ANY future enum growth): describe-schema's hand-listed [Entity, Scalar] array (schema.rs:774) + the "two variants" prose (:388-397) + json_keys (:365) are not derived from the enum, so a new variant is documented into nonexistence — R624's oracle-derived pattern, unapplied. The adjacent rule_class_specs already documents the right pattern in a comment.

THE FACT LEG IS NOT DEAD, IT IS UNLICENSED: if it returns it needs its OWN round, and it must FIRST design the predicate-registry migration primitive (there is no set_predicate / remove_predicate, and stage_registry_entry rejects a divergent re-declare, so today's only migration routes are a vN predicate id or hand-editing the store — both CLAUDE.md hard bans), and it must explicitly argue against R616 decision C rather than build through it. R493's rule is not met: 16/16 targets resolve, 0 dangling in the wild.

THE STATE-EXPRESSION SEAM, named not silent: the threshold 3 lives in the store as prose (f-tide-slips) and in the chart as a magic number, bound by nothing. Per this round's ruling the counter is pinion's and the threshold rides with it, so there is no build today (n=1, no measured fault, R493) — but if a second consumer wants a store-declared threshold, this is the seam to revisit.

SEND THE RULING — it is worthless in our ledger. R585 built describe-schema BECAUSE this consumer reported this class of gap and it still did not reach them; R620's lesson is that the fix is ROUTING, not artifacts. The 3c answer must reach the-tide-that-counts, and their knots.py deserves a pre-commit hook (README documents it as a manual gate, and .githooks holds only commit-msg).

R547's delegation clause rests on a mis-citation of R546 (R546 put cond-guards on the world-state side; R547 restated that as "cond = executable behavior"), so R560 is later, precise, and correct: a guard CONDITION over world-state is Mnemosyne's; only side-effecting execution is SCE.

THE RULING APPLIED to the consumer's 9 MEASURED conds (not to the hypothetical): 7/9 mouth_confessed are ALREADY store-derived through their own seam (store opened_by+completed_by then knots.py then knots.json then engine then event payload then cond; the derived artifact byte-matches a regeneration from the pristine store) = they already implement R560 correctly, UNAIDED, and it is NOT drift; 1/9 on_the_flat comes from tide-table.json with zero store reads = adaptation-side, their call; 1/9 unanswered >= 3 is an ACCUMULATING COUNTER, which R560's OWN carve-out puts pinion-side/optional/deferred = their hand-authored cond is CORRECT, not drift. THEREFORE NO MNEMOSYNE BUILD for gap 3c: the one class they cannot write is exactly the class R560 already assigns to pinion.

3c's PREMISE stands CONFIRMED, not refuted (I had it backwards, and one reviewer with me): it asked about the STATE-EXPRESSION class ("if trust >= N"), not about gates. The consumer authored the 16 opened_by gates on 07-14 and wrote 3c on 07-15 — they knew what they had written the day before, and scoped 3c precisely to the class they could NOT write. Measured: f-tide-slips carries the "three unanswered calls" law as PROSE ONLY with typed leg None; the 852-fact store holds ZERO numeric typed scalars; 15 facts whose prose says "three" are all untyped.

THE CONSUMER WAS A DECIDER, NOT A VICTIM — my framing was wrong and their source says so. opened_by's scalar is a DOCUMENTED decision citing OUR OWN ruling (knots.py:13-18 + build-store.sh: "which fact opens this game's knot is the adaptation consumer's concern, so it is not a place to grow the schema (GAP-005 decision C) ... this gate closes that gap"), and they shipped the gate (knots.py G4 = verbatim my proposed check, fail-loud) in the SAME COMMIT (ed4f134) that introduced the predicate. The gap never existed. Their G5 (is the opening fact diggable before the ritual) is STRONGER than my proposal: existence never broke, satisfiability did.

LEDGER CORRECTION (6th of the R621 class, load-bearing): R620 wrote "the consumer ... never used requires" and used it to ground "do NOT build a condition primitive yet". FALSE — 14 typed uses. The store is TRACKED; at the consumer commit contemporaneous with the field report (da060bd, one hour before it) requires=14 and opened_by=16. R620 measured typed 9.7% (83/852) correctly off that same store and got this item wrong. R620's other two grounds (typed 9.7%, surface.object 0/1000) SURVIVE, so the re-author-then-re-measure order stands.

MY OWN BRIEF'S ERRORS, recorded rather than quietly dropped: the founding premise ("R560 reversed R547") re-asserted the exact reading R621 retracted ONE ROUND EARLIER = the R620 shape repeated by its own corrector; "half-enforced invariant" was a MISCITATION (the typed leg has 3 write paths all routed through build_typed_claim, parity HOLDS, and the scalar leg does exactly what it documents); provenance wrong (opened_by = ed4f134 07-14, not ba907a3 07-13; ba907a3 was requires); "3 gates depend on f-jiun-bloodline" was 1 (I read the subject's outbound count as the target's inbound).
publishable_verification_bullets: EVERY reviewer claim re-run BY ME before it entered this ledger (R620's failure mode was writing an unverified claim; a reviewer is not an oracle either). Two reviewer claims were REJECTED on that re-run: one held R620's "never used requires" referred to the report's content rather than the store (the verbatim R620 text — "the consumer ... has typed 9.7% of facts, used surface.object 0 times, and never used requires" — is about the store, and the third reviewer independently agreed with me); one held there is "no charter conflict to rule on", which overreads R621 CORRECTION 5 ("never perceiving the conflict with R547's body" concedes the conflict).

MEASURED on the real 852-fact consumer store, never a fixture, copied to scratch and never mutating the consumer repo (its git status stayed clean): 9 cond= attrs over 3 vars (mouth_confessed x7, on_the_flat x1, unanswered >= 3 x1); typed 83/852 = 9.7%; predicates requires=14 / opened_by=16 / completed_by=22 / pursues=22; 24 scalar objects split cleanly into 16 that resolve to real fact ids and 8 genuine opaque vocabulary; trust = 0 occurrences store-wide (the consumer's own audit records village-trust as designed-but-not-built) so the RESUME's blocking question rested on a hypothetical.

INJECTION-PROVEN (non-vacuity, the standing rule): add-fact with opened_by pointing at a nonexistent fact = ACCEPTED exit 0 with validate-workspace and validate-continuity both exit 0; the same shape via requires (entity leg) = REJECTED exit 1; retract-fact on a gate's target = SUCCEEDS, leaving the gate dangling, both gates still green. The hole is real. What the review established is that it is NOT a fault the consumer suffers: knots.py G4 catches it fail-loud before engine/ can consume knots.json.

CARRY-1 VERIFIED BY ME e2e, not taken from the reviewers: disclosure overrides hold 1000 fact refs (reader 836 / reader-flee 81 / reader-substitute 83); retract-fact on an override's target SUCCEEDS exit 0 and leaves the override ORPHANED with both validators green; set-disclosure then REFUSES to create that same state ("fact not present in narrative_facts", exit 1). The write path forbids exactly what the delete path creates = the canonical CLAUDE.md anti-pattern, at 1000 refs against my typed leg's 16.

CARRY-2 VERIFIED BY ME e2e on a purpose-built store: a requires edge declared object_kind=scalar IMPORTS CLEAN (exit 0) and report-quest-graph returns prerequisites=[] for the quest that authored it — the edge is SILENTLY DROPPED at the if-let Entity in continuity.rs, while structural_fact_ids matches the predicate STRING and still counts it, so the two disagree. The quest contract's object_shape is prose, not enforcement. Live TODAY, independent of this round.

Citation hygiene (R255): all 18 cited rounds verified present in the changelog before writing. My first check reported R255/R568/R585 ABSENT — a false negative from my own trailing-space prefix, the exact class R620 shipped when list_sections answered "hallucinated" for all 366 rounds. Doubted the tool, re-ran, all 18 present. Ledger 252..624, 371 entries.

Code 0 (the RULEBOOK DESIGN round): no crate, schema, or gate touched. Deliverable = this self-contained entry (R452). Nothing is claimed green that was not run; no build was made, so no build is claimed.
publishable_carry_forward_bullets: NEXT = R626, and it is NOT this round's refuted design: CLOSE THE REAL HALF-ENFORCED INVARIANT — add disclosure overrides to inbound_fact_refs (atomic/lib.rs:4982-5002 scans conflicts_with / supersedes_in_frame / pays_off and never touches disclosure_plans). 1000 live refs, the write path already enforces it, no schema change, no migration, no consumer ask, and it is the brief's own principle applied where it actually holds.

CARRY (live bug, R627 candidate): the if-let Entity silent-drop at continuity.rs:4178/4187 — a requires/pursues edge declared object_kind=scalar imports clean and vanishes from report-quest-graph while structural_fact_ids still counts it. Either enforce quest predicates' object_kind at write time or fail loud on a shape the projection cannot read; do not leave two functions disagreeing.

CARRY (prerequisite for ANY future enum growth): describe-schema's hand-listed [Entity, Scalar] array (schema.rs:774) + the "two variants" prose (:388-397) + json_keys (:365) are not derived from the enum, so a new variant is documented into nonexistence — R624's oracle-derived pattern, unapplied. The adjacent rule_class_specs already documents the right pattern in a comment.

THE FACT LEG IS NOT DEAD, IT IS UNLICENSED: if it returns it needs its OWN round, and it must FIRST design the predicate-registry migration primitive (there is no set_predicate / remove_predicate, and stage_registry_entry rejects a divergent re-declare, so today's only migration routes are a vN predicate id or hand-editing the store — both CLAUDE.md hard bans), and it must explicitly argue against R616 decision C rather than build through it. R493's rule is not met: 16/16 targets resolve, 0 dangling in the wild.

THE STATE-EXPRESSION SEAM, named not silent: the threshold 3 lives in the store as prose (f-tide-slips) and in the chart as a magic number, bound by nothing. Per this round's ruling the counter is pinion's and the threshold rides with it, so there is no build today (n=1, no measured fault, R493) — but if a second consumer wants a store-declared threshold, this is the seam to revisit.

SEND THE RULING — it is worthless in our ledger. R585 built describe-schema BECAUSE this consumer reported this class of gap and it still did not reach them; R620's lesson is that the fix is ROUTING, not artifacts. The 3c answer must reach the-tide-that-counts, and their knots.py deserves a pre-commit hook (README documents it as a manual gate, and .githooks holds only commit-msg).

Round 626 — the disclosure leg of \"a re.decision_summary: R626 closes the REAL half-enforced invariant R625's review surfaced, and adds the escape hatch the guards require. THE DEFECT: apply_disclosure_override refuses an override on a missing fact and refuses a withhold/first_at decision on an untyped one, while retract_fact could DELETE the fact and amend_fact could DROP the typed leg — two mutate paths reaching, from the far side, exactly the states the write path forbids. Four primitives with write authority over one relationship, one enforcer = the CLAUDE.md anti-pattern (the label FITS here; R625 mis-applied it to the typed-object leg, whose parity holds). R508's ledger leans on the guarantee in PROSE — 'the targeted facts are guaranteed typed by the R507 set_disclosure invariant' — and its own gate-side re-check says 'the set_disclosure typed invariant was bypassed': it ANTICIPATED bypass without closing the paths that bypass it. TWO holes, not the one the review named: reviewers found retract; the AMEND hole I found myself by asking what else the write path forbids. FIX: inbound_disclosure_refs (the plan-side referrer scan) wired into both mutate paths, each mirroring the idiom already in that primitive; and disclosure_needs_typed_target as THE one predicate both sides call — the WRITE path was rewired to it, so the two shapes cannot drift into the R295 paste-error. THE SHARPEST SELF-CATCH, and R625's lesson recurring ONE ROUND LATER: there was NO primitive to remove a disclosure override, so the guard would have told authors 'clear the decision first' while making it impossible — permanently un-retracting all 1000 override-carrying facts in the real consumer store, EXACTLY the defect 3 reviewers convicted my R625 design of. A guard without an escape hatch is a trap, not an invariant. So remove_disclosure lands with it (+ CLI + the MCP tool, because MCP is the AI surface of an AI-first substrate and an agent that can create decisions but not clear them is trapped by this very guard). MEASURED not asserted: 1000 refs, of which only 78 are leak-gate-targeted and 922 are re-checked by nothing (so their orphan is silent); my prediction that the amend hole blinds the gate was REFUTED by reading the code (it already fails loud). Both guards proven load-bearing by neutralization; e2e on the real consumer store shows the exact R625 retract now rejecting, the hatch clearing it, and 0 dangling after. 955 tests, clippy-D, fmt, non-breaking.
changes_bullets: THE INVARIANT, now enforced by every write authority instead of one: `apply_disclosure_override` refuses an override on a MISSING fact and refuses a withhold/first_at decision on an UNTYPED one, but `retract_fact` could delete the fact and `amend_fact` could drop the typed leg — reaching from the far side exactly the states the write path forbids. Four primitives with write authority over one relationship, one enforcer = the CLAUDE.md half-enforced-invariant anti-pattern (this time the label FITS; R625 mis-applied it to the typed-object leg, whose parity holds).

R508's ledger LEANS ON THE GUARANTEE IN PROSE and the guarantee was false: "the targeted facts are guaranteed typed by the R507 set_disclosure invariant (re-checked fail-loud)". R508 built both the invariant and a defensive gate-side re-check whose error text literally says "the set_disclosure typed invariant was bypassed" — it anticipated bypass without closing the paths that bypass it.

TWO holes, not the one the R625 review named. Reviewers found retract; I found the AMEND hole myself by asking what ELSE the write path forbids: amending a fact to drop its typed leg while a live withhold rides on it = EXIT 0, leaving a withhold override on an untyped fact. Both are now refused, each mirroring the idiom already in that primitive ("retract or amend the referrers first" / "amend the successor first").

ONE PREDICATE, NOT TWO COPIES: `disclosure_needs_typed_target(mode, has_first_at)` is THE answer to "does this decision need a typed target", and the WRITE path was rewired to call it (it previously inlined the condition). Both sides now ask the identical question over primitive inputs, so the shapes (a decision's args vs a stored override) cannot drift into the R295 paste-error — the anti-pattern that produced the 906-char/cap-200 split.

THE ESCAPE HATCH I NEARLY SHIPPED WITHOUT — the sharpest self-catch, and it is R625's lesson recurring ONE ROUND LATER. There was NO primitive to remove a disclosure override (no remove_disclosure/clear_disclosure anywhere; CLI had only add-disclosure-plan/set-disclosure/report/validate). So my guard would have told authors "clear the disclosure decision first" while making that impossible — permanently un-retracting every override-carrying fact (1000 in the real consumer store). That is EXACTLY the defect 3 reviewers convicted my R625 design of (a migration step unexecutable because the registry had no remover). A guard without an escape hatch is a trap, not an invariant.

NEW: `remove_disclosure` (+ CLI `remove-disclosure`, + the MCP tool). Mirrors `remove_section_binding`/`remove_section` (R267)/`remove_inventory_entry` (R274): --reason mandatory, NotFound on an absent telling OR an absent decision (no silent no-op), the fact untouched (a disclosure decision belongs to the TELLING, R506). The MCP leg is NOT optional surface parity: MCP is the AI surface and this is an AI-first substrate, so an agent that can create decisions but not clear them is trapped by the very guard this round adds.

ROUTING is free here, and that is R622 paying off: the new verb rides the ONE `COMMANDS` table, so the front door names it by construction — no hand-maintained list to drift (R620's class). No tool/verb COUNT was hardcoded anywhere, so adding a tool drifts nothing.
verification_bullets: MY OWN HYPOTHESIS REFUTED BY READING THE CODE, before it could reach the ledger: I predicted the amend hole would silently BLIND the leak gate (the R510 F5 vacuous-pass class). FALSE — `disclosure_leak` already returns Err on a targeted-but-untyped fact ("the set_disclosure typed invariant was bypassed"). So the amend hole is a DEFERRED fail-loud, not silent corruption. The retract orphan IS silent, but only for the untargeted class.

MEASURED, so the severity claim is not inflated: the real consumer store holds 1000 override->fact refs (reader 836 / reader-flee 81 / reader-substitute 83) = state 928 / hint 51 / imply 21, withhold 0, with 78 carrying a first_at pin. Only those 78 are TARGETED (re-checked by the leak gate); the other 922 are re-checked by NOTHING, so their orphan is silent. The fact R625 orphaned e2e (f-after-dark-indoors, mode state, no pin) is in the silent 922.

LOAD-BEARING PROVEN BY NEUTRALIZATION, not by a green test: with the retract leg neutralized the new test FAILS; with the amend guard neutralized it FAILS; restored, it passes. A passing test is not evidence a guard bites.

THE PARITY TEST IS R305-SHAPED and its ACCEPT cases are the point: same edge input through both authorities must agree. Rejects — withhold+untyped, first_at+untyped, retract under either override class. Accepts (the not-over-broad control) — a craft-only `state` with no pin is legal on an untyped fact at the WRITE path, so an amend dropping the typed leg under one stays legal, and an amend that KEEPS the typed leg under a withhold stays legal. Without the accept cases the guard could be over-broad and still look green.

E2E ON THE REAL 852-FACT CONSUMER STORE (scratch copy; the consumer repo was never mutated, git status stayed clean): the EXACT retract R625 proved silently orphaned an override now rejects naming the telling (EXIT 1, was EXIT 0 + silent orphan); `remove-disclosure` clears the decision; the retract then succeeds with dangling overrides = 0; the untouched store still passes validate-workspace and validate-continuity.

GREEN: workspace build, 955 tests passing / 0 failures (954 at R624 + the new parity pin), clippy --workspace --all-targets -D warnings = 0, cargo fmt --check = 0. Citation hygiene (R255): every cited round verified present before writing.

NON-BREAKING by construction and by measurement: the guards fire only on retract/amend of a fact carrying an override — `import_facts` (the consumer's actual build path) cannot replace a fact (3-way absent/identical/divergent) and routes overrides through the same enforcer, so no authoring path newly rejects. The full suite is the negative control.

The quest contract's `object_shape` (schema.rs) is prose, not enforcement. Either enforce quest predicates' object_kind at write time or fail loud on a shape the projection cannot read; do not leave the disagreement.

changes_bullets: R627 is a DEBT round: the owner's cost-no-object session review (3 independent adversarial reviewers on R625+R626) returned SURVIVES-WITH-CORRECTIONS 3/3, and the RULEBOOK forbids deferring a real defect as a separate item. DEFECT 1 (the sharpest, and it inverts R626's own thesis): R626's retract refusal reported disclosure referrers and then gave FACT-class advice — "retract or amend the referrers first". A disclosure referrer is a TELLING: `retract-fact --fact reader` answers "not found: fact_id `reader` not present". So the round whose thesis is "a guard with no escape hatch is a trap" BUILT the hatch and then wrote a sign pointing away from it — on 836 of the consumer's 852 facts, 53 of which emit both classes rendered identically as "referrers". FIXED: the refusal now splits by class and each clause names the primitive that actually clears it (`remove-disclosure --telling <id> --fact <id> --reason <text>`).

DEFECT 2 (a vacuous assertion INSIDE the parity pin whose whole purpose is preventing paste-errors): R626's negative control named `f-untyped-ok`, a fact it never added — the line occurred exactly once in the repo — so it passed on "fact not present in narrative_facts", asserted an ERROR under a comment claiming ACCEPTANCE, and was the test's only bare unwrap_err() with no message check. The WRITE half of the R305 accept-parity leg was therefore untested. Neutralizing either guard left it passing, so R626's neutralization proof could not have caught it. FIXED: the fact is added and the write path must ACCEPT it.

DEFECT 3: R626's test pinned only the message LABEL ("via disclosure override" + "never dangles") and so LOCKED IN the impossible advice of DEFECT 1. FIXED: the pin now asserts the REMEDY, and it is load-bearing — reverting the message to R626's wording FAILS the test.

DEFECT 4: `remove_disclosure` was NOT neutral and R626 shipped that unsaid — clearing an override drops the fact to the plan's `default_mode`, which defaults to `withhold` and IS withhold in all 3 plans of the real consumer store, so clearing a `state` decision silently flips a fact from told to never-told. R626's own prescribed workflow (clear, then retract) leaves it silently withheld if the author stops after step 1. FIXED: the receipt now names the resulting effective mode ("now rides the plan default: withhold"), and the docstring / CLI note / MCP description all say so.

DEFECT 5 (R620's class, one round after R624 built a gate against it): the routed authority did not know the new invariant existed — `describe-schema`'s hand-maintained invariant list had no row for it, while MCP onboarding routes "write-time invariants" there. FIXED: a `disclosure-ref-integrity` row lands, and `disclosure-needs-typed` now states it holds on EVERY write authority. The deeper fix (deriving the invariant list from an oracle rather than hand-maintaining it, the R624 pattern) is NOT done here and is named in the carry — a hand-maintained list is the drift class R620 convicted.

DEFECT 6: the doc comment carrying "the fourth write authority" was a sentence FRAGMENT attached to `inbound_disclosure_refs` — a READ-ONLY helper — so it read as though the scan were a write authority. FIXED, and the census is now stated: the four are `set_disclosure` and `import_facts` (both routed through `apply_disclosure_override`, the only enforcer) plus `retract_fact` and `amend_fact`.

Branches / entities / frames / predicates have NO remover at all, so those legs are safe by ABSENCE, not enforcement — the day either lands they open silently. LOAD-BEARING PROVEN BY NEUTRALIZATION for both new pins, because a passing test is not evidence: making the amend guard OVER-BROAD (block any override, not just typed-needing ones) FAILS the new accept leg with the exact message; reverting the retract message to R626's impossible wording FAILS the remedy pin. STANDING LESSON THIS ARC EARNED (R620->R627, seven rounds): my first design is refuted, my label for the defect is wrong, and my own friction is a consumer defect I normalized. Independent adversarial review before AND after build is not a cost line — it is the only thing that has caught any of it. Reviewers are not oracles either: re-run every claim (2 rejected in R625; two reviewer parse-bugs in R627's review).

Round 629 — the contract derives its voc.decision_summary: R629 makes `describe-schema` DERIVE its enum vocabularies instead of hand-listing them — the authority R620 designated against drift was itself drifting. PROVEN, not argued: adding a 4th `RuleClass` variant and satisfying only what the compiler demanded left the workspace compiling clean, 293 tests green, and the contract SILENTLY OMITTING the new class. THREE comments asserted a safety that did not exist, one of them on the enum's own definition ("an added class breaks its exhaustive match ... instead of silently going undescribed"); half of each was true and it was the wrong half — the match forces a DESCRIPTION, nothing forced the ENUMERATION. MY OWN DESIGN WAS REFUTED BEFORE BUILD by an independent reviewer: I proposed adding `strum` on the premise that "pure Rust cannot force enum enumeration without a macro". FALSE — serde's derive ALREADY writes every variant's published tag and hands the list over through the `Deserializer::deserialize_enum(name, variants, visitor)` TRAIT SIGNATURE, an API contract rather than an error-message format. strum would have been a SECOND derive macro regenerating what the first already generates on these exact types, AND it picks the wrong spelling: the contract publishes serde tags (`withhold`), strum yields Rust variants (`Withhold`), leaving `as_str()` — whose doc CLAIMS serde parity — hand-written and unenforced. The serde oracle closes the enumeration axis and the spelling axis at once, with zero new dependencies. ALL FOUR producers now derive through it (not the two I first scoped), the exhaustive matches are KEPT because they force a gloss per variant, and the TEST — which hardcoded the same four lists and so agreed with the producers at the stale value — no longer re-pins membership (that would move the hand-list into the test) but pins what is NOT tautological: vacuity, serde round-trip, and `as_str()` parity with the published tag. SEVEN variants the authority never published (`IntervalOp` ge/le/eq/gt/lt, `ExclusiveKey` subject/object) existed only as hand-typed strings inside a prose blob and now have derived vocabularies: 5 vocabularies, was 3. VERIFIED against the exact injection that beat the old code: the new variant now reaches the contract with zero hand-edits, because there is no array to forget. DEBT-C STAYS OPEN, re-scoped — closing it would have been a cop-out I nearly wrote into a ledger correction: `invariants()` is underivable only because `AtomicMutateError::Validation(String)` is stringly-typed and collapses 11 invariants into one variant; that is a defect in the reject path, not a category difference, and typing it is structural, not the semantic matching CLAUDE.md rules out. 955 tests, clippy-D 0, fmt.
changes_bullets: THE DEFECT, PROVEN NOT ARGUED: `describe-schema` is the authority R620 designated after a real consumer read our surfaces and concluded seven present capabilities were absent. Four of its producers enumerated their enums from HAND-WRITTEN ARRAYS. Adding a 4th `RuleClass` variant and satisfying only what the compiler demanded left the workspace compiling clean, 293 tests green, and the contract SILENTLY OMITTING the new class. The authority against drift was drifting.

THREE COMMENTS ASSERTED A SAFETY THAT DID NOT EXIST — and one sat on the enum's own definition. "an added class fails to compile HERE, so a new rule can never go undescribed" (schema.rs), "an added DisclosureMode variant fails to compile HERE (and in the variant array below)" (the most explicit lie — it names the array), and RuleClass's own doc: "an added class breaks its exhaustive match (the single-source drift guard) instead of silently going undescribed". Half of each was true and it was the wrong half: the match forces a DESCRIPTION; nothing forced the ENUMERATION. All three deleted and replaced with what is actually enforced (R611's rule: never state an unproven assumption as fact in a comment).

THE FIX IS DERIVATION, NOT A NEW DEPENDENCY — and my own design was REFUTED before build by an independent reviewer. I proposed adding `strum` on the premise that "pure Rust cannot force enum enumeration without a macro". FALSE: serde's derive ALREADY writes every variant's published tag and hands the list over through the `Deserializer::deserialize_enum(name, variants, visitor)` TRAIT SIGNATURE — an API contract, not an error-message format. strum would have been a SECOND derive macro regenerating what the first already generates, on these exact types.

STRUM WOULD ALSO HAVE PICKED THE WRONG SPELLING, which is the sharper half: the contract publishes SERDE tags (`withhold`), strum yields Rust variants (`Withhold`), so the bridge (`as_str()`, whose doc CLAIMS "matches the serde representation") stays hand-written and unenforced. serde capture returns the published string BY CONSTRUCTION, closing the enumeration axis and the spelling axis in one move.

ALL FOUR PRODUCERS now derive through one oracle (`serde_variants` + `variant_from_tag`), not the two I first scoped: `disclosure_mode_values`, `payoff_expectation_values`, `predicate_object_kind_values`, `rule_class_specs`. The exhaustive `match` in each caller is KEPT — it forces a gloss per variant, which was always the true half of those comments. Round-tripping the tag back through serde means no hand-written tag-to-variant table replaces the hand-written array.

THE TEST WAS A SECOND MIRROR and is now a real pin. It hardcoded all four lists, so both sides agreed at the stale value and adding a variant left the contract short AND the test green. Membership assertions are now tautological (the producer derives), so they are DELETED rather than re-pinned in the test — moving a hand-list from production into a test is not a fix. What is pinned instead is NOT tautological: vocabularies are non-empty (vacuity), every published class round-trips through serde, and every enum's `as_str()` AGREES with the serde tag the contract publishes — a live production mirror (receipts, CLI/MCP json) whose doc claims parity that nothing enforced until now.

SEVEN VARIANTS THE AUTHORITY NEVER PUBLISHED now have vocabularies: `IntervalOp` (ge/le/eq/gt/lt) and `ExclusiveKey` (subject/object) existed ONLY as hand-typed strings inside a prose blob (`"op": "ge"|"le"|…`). An author reading the contract could not enumerate them. Both derive through the same oracle.
verification_bullets: THE FIX IS PROVEN AGAINST THE EXACT INJECTION THAT BEAT THE OLD CODE. Before: add `RuleClass::Probe`, satisfy the matches, leave the array → contract omits it, 293 tests green. After: add the variant, the compiler still forces the gloss, and the contract carries it with ZERO hand-edits to any list — `['exclusive','transition','interval','probe']` — BECAUSE THERE IS NO ARRAY TO FORGET. That is the difference between detection and derivation (R622's rule).

THE ORACLE CARRIES A VACUITY GUARD, because the failure mode it prevents is its own: a capturer that silently returned `[]` would make every vocabulary read as "no values" and every downstream check pass vacuously (the R510 F5 class). It asserts non-empty and fails loud.

MEASURED FROM THE CONSUMER'S POSITION, not from a unit test: `describe-schema --json` now publishes 5 vocabularies (was 3) — disclosure_mode [withhold, state, hint, imply], payoff_expectation [unmarked, expected], predicate_object_kind [entity, scalar], interval_op [ge, le, eq, gt, lt], exclusive_key [subject, object].
GREEN: 955 workspace tests / 0 failures, clippy --workspace --all-targets -D warnings = 0, fmt, validate-workspace. Citation hygiene (R255): all cited rounds verified present.

MY OWN CLAIMS THAT THE REVIEW KILLED, recorded because the arc's discipline is to record them: (1) "pure Rust cannot force enum enumeration without a macro" — false, and it was the design's whole premise; (2) "the workspace has a deliberately small dep set (serde, toml, tera, regex, anyhow, thiserror)" — false, the workspace already carries rocksdb, salsa, tonic+prost, tokio, 5 opentelemetry crates, rustls, uuid, sha2 (true only of `mnemosyne-validate`), so my anti-dependency argument was weaker than I stated; (3) "two hand-maintained arrays" — four; (4) "one false comment" — three. The dep question was ultimately decided on DERIVATION vs DUPLICATION, not on dep-count — which is why the corrected premise did not change the verdict, only the reasoning.
carry_forward_bullets: DEBT-C STAYS OPEN, RE-SCOPED — closing it here would have been a cop-out, and R628's framing of it was wrong in a way I nearly wrote INTO a correction. `invariants()` is still hand-maintained (guarded only by a non-empty assert), and I was going to record that it is "not derivable, no enum exists, and checking it would be semantic matching = out of scope per CLAUDE.md". The reviewer refuted that: "no enum to derive from" describes a DEFECT IN THE REJECT PATH, not a category difference — `AtomicMutateError::Validation(String)` is stringly-typed, so all 11 invariants collapse into ONE variant. Name each reject and `invariants()` derives exactly like the other five. "Does each named invariant have a named reject constructor" is STRUCTURAL; CLAUDE.md's T3/T4 exclusion covers "Round NNN actually decides this code", which is a different question. DEBT-C = type the reject path, then derive the invariant list.

RESIDUAL, NAMED NOT SILENT (the oracle cannot close it): `vocabularies()` is a hand-picked list of WHICH enums the contract publishes. There is no way to enumerate "every enum in the crate", and not every enum belongs in an authoring contract — it is an editorial choice. The oracle guarantees a listed vocabulary can never be SHORT; a vocabulary missing ENTIRELY is still possible, which is exactly how interval_op/exclusive_key hid for so long. Documented at the function.

OUT OF SCOPE, NAMED so the next round does not rediscover it: ~17 `from_tag`/`FromStr` sites end in `_ => None`, so an added variant serializes fine but fails to PARSE BACK — the same defect one layer down, and the compiler will not say so. And ~30 prose mirrors (CLI `usage:` strings, MCP tool descriptions, atomic's `expected one of: …` messages) hand-list enum values. The R625 ruling was SENT 2026-07-16 — their answer decides DEBT-F.
Round 629 — the contract derives its voc.publishable_decision_summary: R629 makes `describe-schema` DERIVE its enum vocabularies instead of hand-listing them — the authority R620 designated against drift was itself drifting. PROVEN, not argued: adding a 4th `RuleClass` variant and satisfying only what the compiler demanded left the workspace compiling clean, 293 tests green, and the contract SILENTLY OMITTING the new class. THREE comments asserted a safety that did not exist, one of them on the enum's own definition ("an added class breaks its exhaustive match ... instead of silently going undescribed"); half of each was true and it was the wrong half — the match forces a DESCRIPTION, nothing forced the ENUMERATION. MY OWN DESIGN WAS REFUTED BEFORE BUILD by an independent reviewer: I proposed adding `strum` on the premise that "pure Rust cannot force enum enumeration without a macro". FALSE — serde's derive ALREADY writes every variant's published tag and hands the list over through the `Deserializer::deserialize_enum(name, variants, visitor)` TRAIT SIGNATURE, an API contract rather than an error-message format. strum would have been a SECOND derive macro regenerating what the first already generates on these exact types, AND it picks the wrong spelling: the contract publishes serde tags (`withhold`), strum yields Rust variants (`Withhold`), leaving `as_str()` — whose doc CLAIMS serde parity — hand-written and unenforced. The serde oracle closes the enumeration axis and the spelling axis at once, with zero new dependencies. ALL FOUR producers now derive through it (not the two I first scoped), the exhaustive matches are KEPT because they force a gloss per variant, and the TEST — which hardcoded the same four lists and so agreed with the producers at the stale value — no longer re-pins membership (that would move the hand-list into the test) but pins what is NOT tautological: vacuity, serde round-trip, and `as_str()` parity with the published tag. SEVEN variants the authority never published (`IntervalOp` ge/le/eq/gt/lt, `ExclusiveKey` subject/object) existed only as hand-typed strings inside a prose blob and now have derived vocabularies: 5 vocabularies, was 3. VERIFIED against the exact injection that beat the old code: the new variant now reaches the contract with zero hand-edits, because there is no array to forget. DEBT-C STAYS OPEN, re-scoped — closing it would have been a cop-out I nearly wrote into a ledger correction: `invariants()` is underivable only because `AtomicMutateError::Validation(String)` is stringly-typed and collapses 11 invariants into one variant; that is a defect in the reject path, not a category difference, and typing it is structural, not the semantic matching CLAUDE.md rules out. 955 tests, clippy-D 0, fmt.
publishable_changes_bullets: THE DEFECT, PROVEN NOT ARGUED: `describe-schema` is the authority R620 designated after a real consumer read our surfaces and concluded seven present capabilities were absent. Four of its producers enumerated their enums from HAND-WRITTEN ARRAYS. Adding a 4th `RuleClass` variant and satisfying only what the compiler demanded left the workspace compiling clean, 293 tests green, and the contract SILENTLY OMITTING the new class. The authority against drift was drifting.

THREE COMMENTS ASSERTED A SAFETY THAT DID NOT EXIST — and one sat on the enum's own definition. "an added class fails to compile HERE, so a new rule can never go undescribed" (schema.rs), "an added DisclosureMode variant fails to compile HERE (and in the variant array below)" (the most explicit lie — it names the array), and RuleClass's own doc: "an added class breaks its exhaustive match (the single-source drift guard) instead of silently going undescribed". Half of each was true and it was the wrong half: the match forces a DESCRIPTION; nothing forced the ENUMERATION. All three deleted and replaced with what is actually enforced (R611's rule: never state an unproven assumption as fact in a comment).

THE FIX IS DERIVATION, NOT A NEW DEPENDENCY — and my own design was REFUTED before build by an independent reviewer. I proposed adding `strum` on the premise that "pure Rust cannot force enum enumeration without a macro". FALSE: serde's derive ALREADY writes every variant's published tag and hands the list over through the `Deserializer::deserialize_enum(name, variants, visitor)` TRAIT SIGNATURE — an API contract, not an error-message format. strum would have been a SECOND derive macro regenerating what the first already generates, on these exact types.

STRUM WOULD ALSO HAVE PICKED THE WRONG SPELLING, which is the sharper half: the contract publishes SERDE tags (`withhold`), strum yields Rust variants (`Withhold`), so the bridge (`as_str()`, whose doc CLAIMS "matches the serde representation") stays hand-written and unenforced. serde capture returns the published string BY CONSTRUCTION, closing the enumeration axis and the spelling axis in one move.

ALL FOUR PRODUCERS now derive through one oracle (`serde_variants` + `variant_from_tag`), not the two I first scoped: `disclosure_mode_values`, `payoff_expectation_values`, `predicate_object_kind_values`, `rule_class_specs`. The exhaustive `match` in each caller is KEPT — it forces a gloss per variant, which was always the true half of those comments. Round-tripping the tag back through serde means no hand-written tag-to-variant table replaces the hand-written array.

THE TEST WAS A SECOND MIRROR and is now a real pin. It hardcoded all four lists, so both sides agreed at the stale value and adding a variant left the contract short AND the test green. Membership assertions are now tautological (the producer derives), so they are DELETED rather than re-pinned in the test — moving a hand-list from production into a test is not a fix. What is pinned instead is NOT tautological: vocabularies are non-empty (vacuity), every published class round-trips through serde, and every enum's `as_str()` AGREES with the serde tag the contract publishes — a live production mirror (receipts, CLI/MCP json) whose doc claims parity that nothing enforced until now.

SEVEN VARIANTS THE AUTHORITY NEVER PUBLISHED now have vocabularies: `IntervalOp` (ge/le/eq/gt/lt) and `ExclusiveKey` (subject/object) existed ONLY as hand-typed strings inside a prose blob (`"op": "ge"|"le"|…`). An author reading the contract could not enumerate them. Both derive through the same oracle.
publishable_verification_bullets: THE FIX IS PROVEN AGAINST THE EXACT INJECTION THAT BEAT THE OLD CODE. Before: add `RuleClass::Probe`, satisfy the matches, leave the array → contract omits it, 293 tests green. After: add the variant, the compiler still forces the gloss, and the contract carries it with ZERO hand-edits to any list — `['exclusive','transition','interval','probe']` — BECAUSE THERE IS NO ARRAY TO FORGET. That is the difference between detection and derivation (R622's rule).

THE ORACLE CARRIES A VACUITY GUARD, because the failure mode it prevents is its own: a capturer that silently returned `[]` would make every vocabulary read as "no values" and every downstream check pass vacuously (the R510 F5 class). It asserts non-empty and fails loud.

MEASURED FROM THE CONSUMER'S POSITION, not from a unit test: `describe-schema --json` now publishes 5 vocabularies (was 3) — disclosure_mode [withhold, state, hint, imply], payoff_expectation [unmarked, expected], predicate_object_kind [entity, scalar], interval_op [ge, le, eq, gt, lt], exclusive_key [subject, object].
GREEN: 955 workspace tests / 0 failures, clippy --workspace --all-targets -D warnings = 0, fmt, validate-workspace. Citation hygiene (R255): all cited rounds verified present.

MY OWN CLAIMS THAT THE REVIEW KILLED, recorded because the arc's discipline is to record them: (1) "pure Rust cannot force enum enumeration without a macro" — false, and it was the design's whole premise; (2) "the workspace has a deliberately small dep set (serde, toml, tera, regex, anyhow, thiserror)" — false, the workspace already carries rocksdb, salsa, tonic+prost, tokio, 5 opentelemetry crates, rustls, uuid, sha2 (true only of `mnemosyne-validate`), so my anti-dependency argument was weaker than I stated; (3) "two hand-maintained arrays" — four; (4) "one false comment" — three. The dep question was ultimately decided on DERIVATION vs DUPLICATION, not on dep-count — which is why the corrected premise did not change the verdict, only the reasoning.

Round 630 — remove_section refuses to st.decision_summary: R630 closes DEBT-A+B: `remove_section` gets the referential guard it never had, and DEBT-B's "safe by absence" becomes a tripwire instead of an invisible constraint. THE DEFECT, proven e2e on the real 852-fact consumer store before the fix: remove_section was `sections.remove()` plus a reason check, so it silently orphaned every reference the WRITE path validates — a fact's canon_from/canon_to/evidence, a disclosure decision's first_at pin and surface scene, and a child section's parent_section — with validate-workspace still GREEN. That is R626's own sentence (the write path forbids what the delete path creates) one collection over, and it reaches the SPEC half via parent_section, the co-equal north star. THE FIX: `inbound_section_refs` walks the REAL collections (not a hand list — R629's lesson applied to a referential scan), one clause per field a `sections.contains_key` write-path site already guards; remove_section refuses to strand what the write path refuses to point at a missing section, naming each referrer. THE GUARD NAMES ITS HATCHES (R626's lesson): retract/amend the fact, remove-disclosure the decision, set-section-parent-section --none the child — the refusal message names all three so the remedy is followable, unlike R626's first cut. DEBT-B: entities/branches/frames/predicates are referenced too but have no remover, so those refs are safe by ABSENCE — invisible in the code, which is how it gets lost. A NEARLY-SHIPPED DEFECT: I first encoded that as a const whose comment claimed it "fails to compile until its author writes the scan" — it did no such thing, the exact false-safety-comment class R629 had just convicted; deleted and replaced with `every_remover_pairs_with_an_inbound_scan`, a source tripwire that fails the day a new remove_* identity primitive lands unacknowledged (detection not derivation, R622 — future functions cannot be derived). Both new tests proven load-bearing by neutralization; the strand test carries the hatch path and a not-over-broad negative control. Non-breaking (188 atomic tests unchanged). 957 tests, clippy-D 0, fmt.
changes_bullets: THE DEFECT, PROVEN e2e on the real 852-fact consumer store BEFORE the fix: `remove_section` was `sections.remove()` with a reason check and nothing else. It silently orphaned every reference the WRITE path validates — a fact's canon_from/canon_to/evidence, a disclosure decision's first_at pin and surface scene, and a child section's parent_section — while validate-workspace stayed GREEN (it only trips when a changelog entry coincidentally cites the same section). This is R626's own sentence — the write path forbids what the delete path creates — one collection over, and it reaches the SPEC half (parent_section), the co-equal north star, not just narrative.

THE FIX MIRRORS THE WRITE PATH, DERIVED FROM THE REAL COLLECTIONS not a hand list (R629's lesson, applied to a referential scan). `inbound_section_refs` walks narrative_facts (canon_from/canon_to/evidence), disclosure_plans (first_at/surface.scene), and sections (parent_section) — one clause per field that a `sections.contains_key` site on the write path already guards. What the write path refuses to point at a missing section, remove_section now refuses to strand, naming each referrer and its class.

THE GUARD NAMES ITS HATCHES (the R626 lesson: a guard whose remedy is impossible is a trap). Each class has a real escape: retract/amend the referring fact, clear the disclosure decision (remove-disclosure), re-parent the child (set-section-parent-section, which takes None to detach). The refusal message names all three so the remedy is followable — R626 shipped advice that was impossible for the class it reported, and this round does not repeat it.

DEBT-B TURNED FROM INVISIBLE INTO GUARDED. Entities, branches, frames, predicates are referenced too (surface.object is an entity, a first_at KEY is a branch, a fact's frame/predicate are registry ids) but have NO remover, so those refs are safe by ABSENCE — a safety invisible in the code, which is how it gets lost. A NEARLY-SHIPPED DEFECT: I first encoded this as a `const` whose comment claimed it "fails to compile until its author writes the matching inbound scan" — it does no such thing, the exact false-safety-comment class R629 had just convicted three copies of. Deleted. Replaced with `every_remover_pairs_with_an_inbound_scan`: a source tripwire that fails the day a new `remove_*` identity primitive lands unacknowledged, forcing its author to decide whether it needs a scan. Detection, not derivation (R622) — future functions cannot be derived, so a source tripwire is the honest tool.
verification_bullets: E2E ON THE REAL CONSUMER STORE (scratch copy; the consumer repo was never mutated): `remove-section d15-jeonyeok` — the exact section a first_at pin references, which returned exit 0 + silent orphan at the session's start — now rejects at exit 1 naming the referrers; a canon_from-only section likewise. Non-breaking: 188 pre-existing atomic tests pass (no test removed a referenced section), and the full 957-test workspace is green.

BOTH NEW TESTS PROVEN LOAD-BEARING BY NEUTRALIZATION, because a passing test is not evidence: neutralizing the canon_from scan, the first_at scan, or the parent_section scan each FAILS remove_section_refuses_to_strand_every_reference_class; injecting an unacknowledged `pub fn remove_entity_probe` FAILS the tripwire naming it. Restored, both green. The strand test also carries the escape-hatch path (clear all three classes, then ch-1 removes) and a negative control (an unreferenced section removes without ceremony), so the guard is proven not over-broad.

COVERAGE IS THE WRITE PATH'S MIRROR, checked field by field: every `sections.contains_key`/`.get` site that validates a section ref on the write path (canon_from at 3649, canon_to/evidence at 3663/3683, first_at at 4131/4181, surface scene at 4537, parent_section at 2162) has its inbound clause. A class the write path does not check is deliberately absent (it cannot dangle).

Round 631 — quest object-shape is a stor.decision_summary: R631 closes the `if let Entity` silent-drop, but the fix that landed is NOT the one it started as — an independent reviewer refuted the first cut before commit. The defect: a reserved quest predicate (`pursues`/`requires`) declared object_kind=scalar imported clean, then vanished from report-quest-graph via an `if let Entity` with no else, while structural_fact_ids counted the same fact by predicate string — two readers disagreeing about one fact, exit 0. MY FIRST FIX made quest_graph's arm fail loud — a BAND-AID: structural_fact_ids has an independent caller, report-authoring-frontier with NO --telling, which never runs quest_graph, so the malformed fact was STILL silently miscounted as structural on that path. A read-time guard on one reader is not the fix. THE LANDED FIX is a store-boundary invariant with a single enforcer: check_quest_predicate_shapes (validate layer) rejects any fact whose reserved quest predicate carries the wrong object kind, run in scan_continuity + timeline_gaps AND at the entry of BOTH quest projections (structural_fact_ids, now Result, and quest_graph), so every reader shares ONE invariant and cannot disagree. WHY VALIDATE NOT ATOMIC (reviewer-confirmed): the quest constants are pub(crate) in validate and the dep direction is validate->atomic, so enforcing in the substrate would be a layer violation (quests are projected, no new substrate). THE REQUIRED KIND IS DERIVED FROM THE CONTRACT, not a second hardcoded list (R629's drift class): QuestPredicate gained a machine-readable required_object_kind, describe-schema publishes it, and the guard reads it via schema::quest_predicate_object_kinds — enforced rule and documented contract are one source. Proven: every reader (validate-continuity, report-authoring-frontier no-telling, report-quest-graph) refuses the malformed store exit 1; a healthy store and the real 852-fact consumer store (14 well-formed entity-typed requires facts) pass unchanged (non-breaking); the shared invariant is load-bearing by neutralization. validate-workspace stays exit 0 by design (doc/ledger-only; narrative integrity is validate-continuity's). completed_by correctly unguarded (contract: entity or scalar). 958 tests, clippy-D 0, fmt.
changes_bullets: THE DEFECT, PROVEN e2e: a reserved quest predicate (`pursues`/`requires`) declared object_kind=scalar imported CLEAN and then vanished from report-quest-graph (prerequisites=[]) via an `if let Entity` with no else, while structural_fact_ids counted the same fact by predicate string — two readers disagreeing about one fact, exit 0, no warning.

MY FIRST FIX WAS A BAND-AID, REFUTED BEFORE COMMIT by an independent reviewer: I made quest_graph's `if let` fail loud. But structural_fact_ids has an INDEPENDENT caller — authoring_frontier_report (report-authoring-frontier with NO --telling) calls structural_fact_ids unconditionally but quest_graph only when a telling is passed. So the malformed fact was STILL silently miscounted as structural on that path, exit 0 — the exact failure class the fix claimed to close, one reader over. A read-time guard on one reader is not the fix.

THE ENFORCEMENT MOVED TO A STORE-BOUNDARY INVARIANT, single enforcer, all readers protected by construction. `check_quest_predicate_shapes` (validate layer) rejects any fact whose reserved quest predicate carries the wrong object kind. WHY VALIDATE, NOT ATOMIC (the reviewer confirmed the boundary): the quest predicate constants are pub(crate) in mnemosyne-validate and the dep direction is validate->atomic, so teaching the substrate the reserved quest names would be a layer violation (quests are PROJECTED, no new substrate — ARCHITECTURE). atomic still accepts the fact; validate owns the quest convention and enforces it.

Round 633 — north-star recalibration: a.decision_summary: R633 is a code-0 RECALIBRATION ruling (the R560 boundary-correction precedent): the owner added a THIRD north-star destination on 2026-07-16, alongside spec authoring and narrative authoring — PLAYABLE-narrative production. Definition: when a DIFFERENT AI agent authors a narrative on the substrate, the result comes out playable-ready, problem-free enough to play immediately, and the mechanism is the GATES — from the skeleton (facts/structure) ALONE they filter out what would break at actual play. This RAISES the gate telos from self-consistency to PLAYABILITY: the ai-first doctrine's "gates = self-consistency feedback" is now necessary, not sufficient, because a store with zero timeline contradictions can still be un-playable (a quest's opening fact undiggable before its gate = the consumer's knots.py G5; a canon-order typo making all facts incomparable = DEBT-H; a leaking withhold). #3 is a DISTINCT axis from #2: narrative authoring = can an AI author a coherent branching story at all (capability, proven small-scale R519/R520); playable-narrative = does what it authors come out PLAY-READY with the gate catching play-breakers from the skeleton (output contract + gate completeness). The consumer the-tide-that-counts is exactly a different AI authoring, and its knots.py G4/G5 sidecar fills play-readiness classes our gates lack = the measured #3 gap. This REINTERPRETS the R620-R632 debt arc (R626 disclosure orphan, R630 section strand, R631 quest vanishing, DEBT-H canon-order silent) — under two destinations a session review this turn called them internal-hygiene drift; under #3 they are ON the north star, each a skeleton-looks-fine-play-breaks case. But the reinterpretation does NOT excuse their defects: an incomplete gate (R630 mirrors 3 of 5 ref classes) or a lying one (R631's unbacked cannot-drift claim) is a FALSE playability guarantee, worse under #3. The standing question it opens is gate-suite COVERAGE: not fix-the-next-orphan but "what are the play-time problem classes and does the gate catch each from the skeleton", with the consumer's knots.py as the empirical class catalog. No code, no schema, no gate touched; deliverable = this entry (R452) + the north-star memory update. NEXT = R634 pays the review defects recast as play-readiness completeness, then a code-0 coverage-catalog round.

Round 634 — R630 was a half-measure: the.decision_summary: R634 pays the sharpest defect the owner's cost-no-object session review found: R630 was a HALF-MEASURE, and its own verification claim ("checked field by field: every sections.contains_key site has its inbound clause") was FALSE — inbound_section_refs mirrored 3 of 5 write-validated section-ref classes. THE TWO MISSED CLASSES ARE THE SEVEREST because both are UNFIXABLE once stranded, so there is no hatch to name (the R626 lesson inverted): a branch's fork/converge coordinate (write-validated by build_branch_fork / build_branch_converges per R438/R532 — but there is no remove_branch and a branch is immutable after registration), and a confirmation event's claim section (write-validated by append_confirmation_event per R287 — an append-only ledger). check_store_boundary scans neither, so validate-continuity stayed green over the strand too. IT WAS LIVE IN THE REAL CONSUMER STORE: d06-bam / d08-bam / d12-bam are fork/converge points across the-tide-that-counts' 9 branches, and before this round remove-section on any of them returned exit 0 and permanently stranded a branch; now all three refuse at exit 1 naming the class. UNDER R633's third north-star destination this is not hygiene, it IS the destination — a stranded fork point is exactly "the skeleton looks fine, play breaks", and an INCOMPLETE play-readiness gate is a FALSE playability guarantee, which is why R630's miss mattered more than the classes it covered. Both new classes proven load-bearing by neutralization; the strand test now pins all five classes and keeps its hatch path + not-over-broad control. The tripwire's limit is named rather than papered over: it checks the SET of remover names, so it is structurally incapable of catching an incomplete existing scan — the completeness pin is the strand test, not the tripwire. 958 tests, clippy-D 0, fmt, non-breaking.
changes_bullets: R634 pays the sharpest defect the owner's cost-no-object session review found: R630 was a HALF-MEASURE. It claimed "one clause per field the write path guards" and "checked field by field: every sections.contains_key site has its inbound clause" — that verification claim was FALSE. `inbound_section_refs` mirrored 3 of 5 write-validated section-ref classes.

THE TWO MISSED CLASSES ARE THE SEVEREST, because both are UNFIXABLE once stranded — there is no hatch to name (the R626 lesson inverted): (1) a branch's fork/converge coordinate, validated at write by build_branch_fork / build_branch_converges ("canon coordinates are structure refs", R438/R532) — there is no `remove_branch` and a branch is immutable after registration, so a stranded fork point can never be repaired; (2) a confirmation event's claim section, validated at write by append_confirmation_event (R287 fail-loud) — an append-only ledger, equally unrepairable. `check_store_boundary` scans neither, so validate-continuity stayed green over the strand too.

IT WAS LIVE IN THE REAL CONSUMER STORE, not hypothetical: d06-bam / d08-bam / d12-bam are fork/converge points across the-tide-that-counts' 9 branches. Before this round `remove-section d06-bam` returned exit 0 and permanently stranded the branch. Now all three refuse, exit 1, naming the class.

UNDER R633's THIRD NORTH-STAR DESTINATION this is not hygiene — it is the destination. A stranded fork point is exactly "the skeleton looks fine, play breaks": the world-line's divergence coordinate points at nothing, and no gate said so. An INCOMPLETE play-readiness gate is a FALSE playability guarantee, which is why R630's miss mattered more than the classes it did cover.
verification_bullets: E2E ON THE REAL CONSUMER STORE (scratch copy; their repo never mutated): all three live fork/converge points (d06-bam, d08-bam, d12-bam) now refuse removal at exit 1 naming "via fork point"; before R634 each returned exit 0 and stranded a branch unrepairably.

BOTH NEW CLASSES PROVEN LOAD-BEARING BY NEUTRALIZATION, because a passing test is not evidence: neutralizing the fork/converge scan FAILS the strand test; neutralizing the confirmation-event scan FAILS it; restored, green. The test now covers all FIVE classes (canon_from, canon_to, evidence, first_at, surface scene, parent_section, fork/converge, claim section) and keeps its escape-hatch path + not-over-broad negative control from R630.

THE TWO NEW CLASSES CARRY NO HATCH, and the test says so rather than pretending: unlike facts (retract/amend), disclosure decisions (remove-disclosure) and child sections (set-section-parent-section --none), a fork point and a confirmation claim cannot be cleared — so the refusal IS the defense, and that is why protecting the section is the only correct behavior.

(2) R631's unbacked "cannot drift" claim — `object_shape` prose and `required_object_kind` both state the rule with NO parity test binding them (the R620/R629 class, reintroduced); and its "store boundary / by construction" wording overstates (the malformed fact PERSISTS at write, caught only at scan — verified).

The test now covers all FIVE classes (canon_from, canon_to, evidence, first_at, surface scene, parent_section, fork/converge, claim section) and keeps its escape-hatch path + not-over-broad negative control from R630.

THE TWO NEW CLASSES CARRY NO HATCH, and the test says so rather than pretending: unlike facts (retract/amend), disclosure decisions (remove-disclosure) and child sections (set-section-parent-section --none), a fork point and a confirmation claim cannot be cleared — so the refusal IS the defense, and that is why protecting the section is the only correct behavior.

GREEN: 958 workspace tests / 0 failures, clippy --workspace --all-targets -D warnings = 0, fmt, validate-workspace. Citation hygiene (R255): all cited rounds verified present. Non-breaking: the consumer never calls remove-section (verified R630), and the guard only fires on referenced sections.
publishable_carry_forward_bullets: STILL OWED FROM THE SAME REVIEW (RULEBOOK: same-session defects, and R633 makes them north-star-central): (1) DEBT-H — a malformed canon-order file (top-level key `order` not `edges`) passes SILENTLY with order_nodes=0 and validate-continuity exit 0; fix = `deny_unknown_fields` on CanonOrderFile mirroring the rules-wire, or fail-loud on facts>0 with order_nodes==0. (2) R631's unbacked "cannot drift" claim — `object_shape` prose and `required_object_kind` both state the rule with NO parity test binding them (the R620/R629 class, reintroduced); and its "store boundary / by construction" wording overstates (the malformed fact PERSISTS at write, caught only at scan — verified).

THE TRIPWIRE'S LIMIT, named not silent: `every_remover_pairs_with_an_inbound_scan` checks the SET of remover names, so it fires for a future remover but is structurally incapable of catching an INCOMPLETE existing scan — exactly the defect R634 just fixed. Its weaknesses stand: it reads only this crate's lib.rs and matches the literal `pub fn remove_` prefix. The completeness pin is the strand test's five classes, not the tripwire; do not mistake one for the other.

THEN the R633 COVERAGE ROUND (code-0): reverse-engineer the consumer's knots.py G1-G6 into the catalog of play-time problem classes, map each to a gate we have or lack.
